---
title: "Theorem 2.8 — Law of total variance"
lean_name: "HansenEconometrics.law_total_variance_rv"
chapter: 2
---

## Textbook statement

For a square-integrable response $Y$ and measurable conditioning variable $X$,
Hansen's law of total variance decomposes total variation into residual and
explained components:

$$
\operatorname{Var}(Y)
=
\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)]
+ \operatorname{Var}(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]).
$$

In Lean, the random-variable-facing theorem is
[`law_total_variance_rv`](../../assumptions/chapter2.qmd). The rearranged
backend theorem `variance_decomposition` states the same identity directly for
a sigma-algebra.

### Proof sketch

This is exactly Mathlib's conditional-variance identity:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid\mathcal{G})]
+ \operatorname{Var}(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid\mathcal{G}])
= \operatorname{Var}(Y).
$$

The Chapter 2 wrapper does two things: it specializes $\mathcal{G}$ to
$\sigma(X)$ and rewrites the notation to `condVarOn μ Y X` and
`condExpOn μ Y X`. The rearranged backend theorem flips the equality so it
reads in Hansen's usual direction.

## Lean statement

The public wrapper lives in
[`HansenEconometrics/Chapter2Variance.lean`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter2Variance.lean#L122):

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The variable-facing law of total variance.">theorem law_total_variance_rv</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Response Y and conditioning variable X.">{Y : Ω → ℝ} {X : Ω → β}</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="X is measurable, so sigma(X) is a valid conditioning space below the ambient measurable structure.">(hX : Measurable X)</span>
    [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Y has a finite second moment.">(hY : MemLp Y 2 μ)</span> :
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Expected conditional variance plus variance of the conditional mean equals total variance.">μ[condVarOn μ Y X] + Var[condExpOn μ Y X; μ] = Var[Y; μ]</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

### Reading the statement

| Lean | Math | What it gives us |
|---|---|---|
| `{Y : Ω → ℝ}` | $Y$ | **Given:** response variable. |
| `{X : Ω → β}` | $X$ | **Given:** conditioning variable. |
| `(hX : Measurable X)` | $X$ measurable | Lets Lean condition on $\sigma(X)$. |
| `[IsProbabilityMeasure μ]` | probability measure | Variance is stated under a probability law. |
| `(hY : MemLp Y 2 μ)` | $Y \in L^2$ | Finite second moment. |
| `μ[condVarOn μ Y X]` | $\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)]$ | Residual variance component. |
| `Var[condExpOn μ Y X; μ]` | $\operatorname{Var}(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X])$ | Explained variance component. |
| `= Var[Y; μ]` | $=\operatorname{Var}(Y)$ | Total variance. |

## Translation notes

- `condVarOn μ Y X` is the variable-facing conditional variance wrapper.
- `condExpOn μ Y X` is the variable-facing conditional expectation wrapper.
- The backend theorem
  [`variance_decomposition`](https://github.com/apoorvalal/lean-hansen-econometrics/blob/main/HansenEconometrics/Chapter2Variance.lean#L45)
  states `Var[Y; μ] = μ[Var[Y; μ | m]] + Var[μ[Y | m]; μ]`; it is just the
  same equality in the opposite orientation.

## Tactic-by-tactic walkthrough

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="The public wrapper: condition on the random variable X instead of an arbitrary sigma-algebra.">theorem law_total_variance_rv
    {Y : Ω → ℝ} {X : Ω → β}
    (hX : Measurable X)
    [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    (hY : MemLp Y 2 μ) :
    μ[condVarOn μ Y X] + Var[condExpOn μ Y X; μ] = Var[Y; μ] := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Unfold the variable-facing wrappers and reuse the sigma-algebra theorem with m = sigma(X).">  simpa [condVarOn, condExpOn, conditioningSpace] using</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Apply the backend law of total variance to the conditioning space generated by X.">    law_total_variance
      (m := conditioningSpace X)
      (m₀ := inferInstance)
      (μ := μ)
      (Y := Y)
      (conditioningSpace_le hX)
      hY</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

The backend theorem itself is a direct Mathlib specialization:

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Backend theorem in sigma-algebra form.">theorem variance_decomposition
    {Y : Ω → ℝ}
    (hm : m ≤ m₀)
    [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    (hY : MemLp Y 2 μ) :
    Var[Y; μ] = μ[Var[Y; μ | m]] + Var[μ[Y | m]; μ] := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Flip the equality so Mathlib's theorem can close the goal in its native orientation.">  rw [eq_comm]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Use law_total_variance, which wraps Mathlib's integral_condVar_add_variance_condExp.">  simpa using law_total_variance (m := m) (m₀ := m₀) (μ := μ) (Y := Y) hm hY</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

## Downstream uses

- `variance_condExpOn_le_variance` extracts the explained-variance bound.
- `residualVarOn_antitone` uses the decomposition to show that richer
  conditioning information weakly reduces residual variance.
